title: CV & Selected Work
text:
Selected academic archive
This section will hold a small collection of selected undergraduate and graduate work. These items are included as an academic archive rather than as the main focus of the website.

title: RISE Fellowship
text: I am honored to have been selected for Boise State University’s competitive, NSF-funded Responsibility in Innovation and Scholarship Experience (RISE) traineeship. I enter the fellowship with an interdisciplinary foundation and practical experience examining responsible technology use empirically, collaboratively, and at the institutional level. Beginning in fall 2026, I will build on that preparation through coursework, stakeholder engagement, and university initiatives that connect AI innovation with human values and human flourishing.

title: RISE Fellowship
text:
RISE traineeship
Boise State University’s Responsibility in Innovation and Scholarship Experience (RISE) program is supported by a nearly $2 million award from the U.S. National Science Foundation’s Research Traineeship program under award no. 2509828.
RISE prepares AI graduate researchers to anticipate the potential social harms of emerging technologies, work with the people those technologies may affect, and adapt their work in response. The program brings researchers in computing, engineering, and other STEM disciplines together with faculty from the humanities and social sciences, industry professionals, and community stakeholders. Its goal is to bridge technical AI training with a broader understanding of social issues and human values so that AI research and innovation can contribute to human flourishing.
The traineeship uses a team-science approach that includes coursework, mentoring, stakeholder workshops, roundtable discussions, professional development, and industry experiences. Twenty-five graduate students are expected to complete the full training experience, including fifteen funded trainees, while at least 200 additional Boise State students will participate in individual elements of the program. Through this structure, RISE seeks to strengthen responsible AI education, broaden participation in technology development, and prepare researchers for collaborative work across academia, government, and industry.

title: RISE Fellowship
text:
RISE bootcamp
Before beginning my funded traineeship, I attended the two-day RISE Spring Bootcamp on January 7 and 8, 2026. The bootcamp introduced RISE applicants to Responsible Research and Innovation and provided opportunities to evaluate the societal impacts of AI-related work, learn how government and industry professionals approach ethical AI development, and connect with other researchers interested in responsible innovation.
As part of the bootcamp, I completed a researcher-values exercise examining how different priorities can shape responsible AI. I identified agency as an essential value because people should be able to understand, question, and meaningfully influence how AI affects their work, education, and communities. I also considered the tension between human-centered values and return on investment, particularly when short-term efficiency is prioritized over autonomy, integrity, or long-term social consequences. Finally, I identified tenacity as an easily overlooked value that can help people use AI without surrendering the persistence, judgment, and accountability necessary for learning and trustworthy work.
The bootcamp culminated in an Individual Development Plan connecting my career, skill-development, and project goals. The plan will serve as a living guide for developing interdisciplinary relationships, strengthening my research and communication skills, and contributing to public conversations about responsible AI. When I return for the fall bootcamp, I will revisit these priorities, connect with the incoming RISE cohort, and refine the plan as my traineeship develops.

title: RISE Fellowship
text:
Social Impacts of Computing
Boise State’s Social Impacts of Computing (SioC) community explores and helps shape the evolving relationship between computing and society. Through workshops and transdisciplinary conversations, SioC brings together faculty, students, industry professionals, and community partners to examine how computing affects human flourishing and how competing values can inform responsible innovation.
My involvement has developed across each of the three annual workshops. I attended the inaugural SioC workshop, helped plan and evaluate the second workshop in 2025, and will contribute to the third workshop in September 2026 as part of my RISE fellowship work. This progression has allowed me to move from participating in SioC conversations to helping document what the community discusses, values, and learns.

SioC 2025 workshop analysis
The 2025 workshop, Emerging Technologies and Well-Being, brought together 71 participants from education, business, government, healthcare, technology, finance, and other sectors. The program combined panels and demonstrations with participant-directed unconference discussions about extended reality, education, the environment, healthcare, and artificial intelligence.
Following the workshop, I analyzed registration and attendance data, pre-workshop responses, post-workshop feedback, and participant-generated notes from sticky notes and whiteboards. I used thematic analysis to identify recurring concerns and interests, and network analysis to examine how values appeared together in participants’ descriptions of the social impacts of computing. Responsibility, transparency, and integrity formed the most central cluster of values, while the analysis also identified collaboration, curiosity, privacy, inclusivity, equity, and environmental stewardship as important parts of the community’s conversation. I combined these findings with participation patterns and event feedback to develop recommendations for future SioC activities.


SioC 2026 workshop
The Social Impacts of Computing Workshop 2026 will take place September 17 and 18 at Boise State University. Its sessions will address AI hesitance, AI across education, RISE and AI scholarship, and collaborative problem solving through the Tech Kitchen. Each session will combine an introduction, a cross-sector panel, and audience-led roundtable discussions.
My anticipated role centers on evaluating participant experiences, workshop conversations, and event outcomes. I expect to analyze participant feedback and records of the roundtable discussions, with qualitative thematic analysis serving as an important part of that work. Repeating elements of the 2025 evaluation will also create an opportunity to compare the second and third workshops and examine how participants’ concerns, values, and priorities may have changed over the intervening year.

title: Teaching
text: My teaching is grounded in the belief that students learn best when they are supported through productive struggle, clear expectations, and meaningful opportunities to practice. I am especially interested in how students learn technical skills in the AI era while developing agency, persistence, transparency, and critical judgment.

title: Teaching
text:
DATA-R155 redesign
I teach the foundations of the statistical programming language R in DATA-R155: Introduction to R Programming. This is a one-credit course at Boise State University.
Generative AI has made R code easier to produce. Beginning programmers still need ways to understand what the code is doing, determine whether it worked, and explain how it connects a question to a result.
Since summer 2025 semester, I’ve iteratively redesigned DATA-R155 around a question: How can an introductory programming course help all students make their thinking visible as they learn to work with R and generative AI? My answer was to the learning process part of the documented workflow. The course asks students to explain code, document how their understanding develops, and practice using AI in specific guided ways.
The redesign takes form through two connected instructional resources. A digital open educational resource guides students through R, learning routines, and selected AI practices. A Canvas course environment organizes pacing, assessment, student choice, and submitted work. I designed, authored, built, and teach these resources with instructional-design guidance from Boise State eCampus and professional-development support from the Center for Teaching and Learning.

title: Teaching
text:
Open educational resource
I am designing a living open educational resource (OER). This textbook is publicly available on GitHub and is designed to be iteratively improved based on student feedback and my own teaching experience.

Explainable and reproducible work
The goal is to teach students how to learn R, covering more breadth than depth. Most R programming skills are introduced at the “understanding” level in Bloom’s taxonomy. Students are expected to explain how code works; memorizing functions is not a course expectation. The OER also showcases selected RStudio capabilities, providing students with ideas on how learning R can be extended beyond the course.
The text does not only introduce R navigation, syntax, debugging, data manipulation, and visualization at an introductory level. It’s activities teach formatting for an R script, R Notebook, and Quarto files that place code within a written account of learning reflections. I emphasize reading and explaining code, investigating unfamiliar functions, and leaving a record that makes the work easier to revisit and follow. Students learn how to format their R files into sharable HTML reports.
The design is intended to empower students so that they can independently learn the techniques for their data analytic needs after the course. This approach makes the course applicable to students with a wide range of backgrounds and future goals.


Self-regulated learning routines
Novice programmers need explicit support for problem-solving processes (Loksa et al., 2016). Students learn how to manage confusion and strengthen their metacognitive awareness with the aid of recurring emojis 🎯 Goal, ⚡ Code, ✅ Verify, and 🗣 Reflect. Emojis are used to illuminate the routine.


Learning skills embedded
Along with the learning routine emojis, I use emojis to highlight learning skills that support the development of intellectual virtues (Baehr, 2022). Each module foregrounds a learning skill: 💪 tenacity, ✍️ transparency, 🦁 courage, 🧭 autonomy, 🤔 open-mindedness, 🐱 curiosity, or ✔️ thoroughness. Using the emoji, I model how to document the highlighted skill within the guided activity. Then I prompt students to practice the skill within the practice space.

Goal, Code, Verify, Reflect: Guided Activities for Learning R Workflow — living OER draft; publicly available chapters may change while the course is being taught.

Because the online OER is still being developed and used in an active course, the public chapters change as modules are revised or released to students.

title: Teaching
text:
Canvas learning environment
The Canvas site is the working course environment students use to access modules, complete activities, submit work, and receive feedback. It links the OER to low-stakes core assessments and advanced learning activities.
To support an alignment with outcomes, promote revisions, and encourage students to take ownership of their learning, I’ve carefully designed the assessment structure.

Specifications grading
Research on feedback and assessment shaped this choice. Self-regulated learning routines promote the use of feedback to revise work, but grades can redirect students’ attention away from comments and toward feelings of judgment. The presence and value of a numerical grade can also shape how students perceive written feedback (Black et al., 2004; Lancaster et al., 2020). Because mastery-oriented assessment can change the meaning of assessment (Smeding et al., 2013), I selected specifications grading to create conditions for self-regulated learning. This grading system offers one structure for aligning assessment with mastery, feedback use, and revision (Nilson & Packowski, 2025).
This approach organizes the course around work evaluated as ✅ Complete or 🟡 Not Ready. All students complete a shared 🌱 core to earn a passing grade (C), then select from a larger set of 🌳 advanced activities to earn a higher grade (B/A). Completing activities unlocks more activity choices, allowing students to decide where they want to extend their learning and what work they will undertake toward their intended grade.


AI as a resource for learning
LLMs may interfere with novice self-regulation when students move directly from prompt to generated solution, bypassing planning and reflection (Prasad & Sane, 2024).
I designed guided activities that help students use generative AI to investigate code and test how well they can explain it. Students are offered several custom course chatbots that can answer questions about R, the course, and provide code as part of specific guided activities. Additionally, I created a few custom bots for myself to use for feedback and student communication.
Many activities offer optional AI-use guidance. For example, one activity asks students to explain a provided R script. Students can choose whether an AI tool or a classmate will reconstruct the code from that explanation. The reconstruction makes ambiguities in the explanation visible. Similar choices appear throughout the course, giving students a choice between working with a peer or an AI system.


Instructional design
I designed, authored, built, and teach the course with instructional-design guidance from Boise State eCampus and professional-development support from the Center for Teaching and Learning. I also participated in Boise State’s faculty learning communities “Designing for Student Success” and “Accessible by Design,” applying that work to the structure and presentation of DATA-R155.
The Canvas course environment offers multiple ways for students to navigate the course, including through a website-like page design, a module-based structure, and a weekly calendar. A consistent weekly structure helps students know what to expect and how to plan their work. I use Canvas Learning Mastery Gradebook, custom letter grade, and module requirements to support specifications grading and assignment access. The Canvas sandbox can be used for either online or in-person course delivery.
This redesign is one place where my teaching and research meet.

title: Teaching
text:
Scholarship of Teaching and Learning
The associated Scholarship of Teaching and Learning study will examine students’ experiences with the design and how it relates to course performance, later enrollment, and the learning practices visible in their work. The study asks how student success outcomes and equity patterns in redesigned DATA-R155 offerings compare with historical DATA-R155 offerings and a comparison course, and to what extent students continue into the R Certificate or subsequent R coursework.
Specifications grading is meant to create conditions for better learning behavior, but it does not guarantee it. Students may still focus on grades, resist the lack of partial credit, complete revisions superficially, or experience anxiety when expectations are unfamiliar. Grade-pathway choices, assignment completion, revision records, and selected reflections will help me examine how students navigate mastery, feedback, productive struggle, and course expectations.
In an AI-assisted introductory R course, the need for process evidence becomes sharper because final code output alone is too weak as evidence of learning. A runnable script, correct table, or polished report may show that a student reached an answer, but it may not show whether the student understood the code, checked the output, used AI appropriately, incorporated feedback, or revised a flawed workflow. The study will examine changes in students’ readiness for further R coursework and look for evidence that they are developing responsible AI-use judgment and a reproducible and explainable R workflow as defined in the course.
I presented the study design in the poster Redesigning DATA-R155 for the AI Era at Boise State’s 2026 Great Ideas for Teaching and Learning Summit.

“Redesigning DATA-R155 for the AI Era” — Boise State University Great Ideas for Teaching and Learning Summit 2026 poster
“Designing for Productive Learning Struggles with AI” — Innovate Idaho 2026 Lighting Talk video

title: Research & Collaborations
text: My current research examines how knowledge, values, and emerging AI practices move through research and learning communities. I am especially interested in how people learn, collaborate, trust, and make responsible decisions with emerging technologies.

title: Research & Collaborations
text:
Human-Centered AI Communities
This section will describe my work with the Idaho Institute for Human-Centered AI and related community-engaged research on AI in education, healthcare, and professional practice.

title: Research & Collaborations
text:
AIROE as previous collaboration
This section will describe AI in Research Optimized Ethically as a previous collaboration that helped shape my interest in generative AI, research integrity, institutional change, and interdisciplinary values.

title: Research & Collaborations
text:
Master’s thesis foundation
My master’s thesis used social network analysis to examine interdisciplinary collaboration in Boise State’s Grand Challenges initiative. This project helped shape my current interest in knowledge networks, collaboration structures, and the conditions that support or limit interdisciplinary research.
Link to thesis will go here.

title: Lorraine Gaudio
text:
Hello World!
After 20-plus years of grooming dogs, I did a 180 and went back to school. I earned a master’s degree in anthropology and am now pursuing a PhD in computing with a focus on knowledge networks, responsible AI, and interdisciplinary collaboration. I am also a RISE Fellow studying how emerging technologies shape research, learning, and human flourishing.

title: Lorraine Gaudio
text:
Hello World!
After 20-plus years of grooming dogs, I did a 180 and went back to school. I earned a master’s degree in anthropology and am now pursuing a PhD in computing with a focus on knowledge networks, responsible AI, and interdisciplinary collaboration. I am also a RISE Fellow studying how emerging technologies shape research, learning, and human flourishing.

title: Lorraine Gaudio
text:
What I study
I study how knowledge, values, and emerging AI practices move through research and learning communities. My work brings together data analytics, social network analysis, mixed methods, and human-centered approaches to emerging technology.

title: Lorraine Gaudio
text:
What I do


Map knowledge networks
I use network thinking to study collaboration, technology adoption, and the movement of practices across communities.


Study AI in practice
I examine how people learn, teach, collaborate, and make decisions with generative AI and other emerging technologies.


Build bridges across fields
I work across computing, anthropology, education, and responsible innovation to support more thoughtful technology use.
